1: A revelation of God: "The Mighty One, God, the LORD, speaks and summons the earth from east to west." The triple divine name — El Elohim YHWH — opens this covenant lawsuit psalm with a theophanic summons of universal scope. The divine judge who "speaks and summons the earth" is the same judge who will summon all nations at the final judgment. John 5:22-23 transfers this judicial authority to Christ: "the Father judges no one, but has entrusted all judgment to the Son, that all may honor the Son just as they honor the Father." The God who summons the earth in Psalms 50:1 summons it through the Son.
2: A revelation of God: "Out of Zion, perfect in beauty, God blazes out." The theophanic blazing of God from Zion — the divine glory manifesting from the covenant city — is the language of theophany that the NT applies to the Parousia. 2 Thessalonians 1:7-8: Christ will be "revealed from heaven in blazing fire with his powerful angels." The "perfect in beauty" of Zion prefigures the "glorious appearing of our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ" (Titus 2:13). The divine blazing from Zion is ultimately the blazing of the Son of God in his eschatological self-revelation.
3: A revelation of God: "Our God comes and will not stay silent; fire devours before him, and a fierce storm rages around him." The advent of the divine judge — coming, not silent, accompanied by fire and storm — is the eschatological arrival pattern. Hebrews 12:29 ("our God is a consuming fire") and 1 Thessalonians 4:16 ("the Lord himself will come down from heaven, with a loud command, with the voice of the archangel and with the trumpet call of God") develop this arrival pattern for the return of Christ. The God who will not stay silent is the Word made flesh who spoke creation into existence and who will speak the final judgment.
4: A theme: "He calls out to the heavens above and to the earth, to judge his people." The judicial summons of heaven and earth as witnesses — the covenant lawsuit structure (rîv) where creation witnesses the case against Israel — is a prophetic form (Isaiah 1:2; Micah 6:1-2). Christ is both the judge of v4 and, in his earthly life, the one judged on behalf of his people: "God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God" (2 Corinthians 5:21). The judgment against God's people that Ps 50 pronounces was absorbed by Christ at the cross.
5: A theme: "Bring together my faithful people, those who sealed covenant with me by sacrifice." The gathering of the covenant community — the hasidim (faithful/devout ones) who formalized the covenant through sacrifice — is the positive counterpart to the judgment on formalism in the psalm. Hebrews 9:15: "Christ is the mediator of a new covenant, that those who are called may receive the promised eternal inheritance — now that he has died as a ransom to set them free from the sins committed under the first covenant." The "sealing by sacrifice" of the old covenant (Exodus 24:8) is the pattern fulfilled in Christ's blood of the new covenant (Matthew 26:28; Hebrews 9:20).
6: A revelation of God: "The heavens declare his righteousness, for God himself is the judge." Creation's testimony to divine righteousness is the cosmological grounding of the psalm's covenant lawsuit. Romans 1:18-20 appeals to the same created witness: "what may be known about God is plain... because God has made it plain... his eternal power and divine nature." But the fullest declaration of divine righteousness is not cosmological but Christological: "God presented Christ as a sacrifice of atonement... to demonstrate his righteousness" (Romans 3:25-26). The heavens that declare YHWH's righteousness in Psalms 50:6 are declaring the righteousness of the judge who is also the atoning sacrifice.
7: A revelation of God: "'Listen, my people, and I will speak; I will testify against you, O Israel. I am God, your God.'" The divine self-identification ("I am God, your God") within the covenant lawsuit is the covenant formula of Sinai (Exodus 20:2 — "I am the LORD your God"). The one who speaks here is the one who gave the Torah at Sinai — and in the NT, this divine speaker is identified with Christ: John 8:58 ("before Abraham was born, I am!"); 1 Corinthians 10:4 ("the spiritual rock that accompanied them... was Christ"). The "I am God, your God" of Psalms 50:7 is the same divine identity that Christ claims throughout John's Gospel in the ego eimi ("I am") declarations.
8: A theme: "'My rebuke is not over your sacrifices; your burnt offerings are always before me.'" YHWH's rebuke is not about the quantity of sacrifice — Israel has been offering. The problem is the misunderstanding of what sacrifice does: it is not a mechanism for feeding or obligating God. Hebrews 10:1 makes the same point about Levitical sacrifice: "the law is only a shadow of the good things that are coming — not the realities themselves. For this reason it can never, by the same sacrifices repeated endlessly year after year, make perfect those who draw near to worship." The sacrificial system was not the endpoint but the pointing-toward of the one sacrifice that does accomplish what all the burnt offerings could not.
9: A theme: "'I do not need a bull from your household or goats from your pens.'" The divine independence from human food-provision — YHWH does not require feeding like the gods of surrounding nations — demolishes the ANE transactional sacrificial model. Psalm 50's critique of the "feeding the gods" conception is the same critique that Acts 17:25 voices in Paul's Areopagus speech: "he is not served by human hands, as if he needed anything, because he himself gives everyone life and breath and everything else." The God who does not need to be fed is the God who instead feeds his people — most fully in Christ, the bread of life (John 6:35).
10: A revelation of God: "'Every animal of the forest is mine — the cattle on a thousand hills.'" Divine ownership of all creation — the total sovereign possession that makes human sacrifice-offering not a gift but a return of what was already his. This is the theological basis for Paul's statement that "the earth is the Lord's, and everything in it" (1 Corinthians 10:26, citing Psalms 24:1). In Christ, the creator's ownership is disclosed in its fullest form: "in him all things were created... and in him all things hold together" (Colossians 1:16-17). The God who owns all the cattle cannot be bribed or compensated; he can only be worshiped.
11: A revelation of God: "'I know every bird in the hills; everything that moves in the open fields is mine.'" Divine omniscience over all creatures — the total knowledge of the creator who tracks every sparrow. Matthew 10:29-31 develops exactly this claim through Christ: "Are not two sparrows sold for a penny? Yet not one of them will fall to the ground outside your Father's care. And even the very hairs of your head are all numbered." The God who knows every bird in Psalms 50:11 is the Father who watches the sparrow in Matthew 10 — and his care for birds is the ground for his far greater care for humans made in his image.
12: A revelation of God: "'If I were hungry, I would not come to you — the world and everything in it is mine.'" The divine independence from creaturely provision — YHWH owns the world — is both a refutation of transactional religion and a revelation of the Creator's self-sufficiency. John 1:3 applies this ownership to the pre-incarnate Word: "through him all things were made; without him nothing was made that has been made." The God who needs nothing from humans is the same God who nonetheless became human in Christ — not because he needed to but because we needed him. Grace, not necessity, drives the incarnation.
13: A theme: "'Do I eat bull meat? Do I drink the blood of goats?'" The rhetorical reductio of sacrificial misunderstanding — YHWH eating and drinking the animal offerings — is the clearest OT demolition of the ANE gods-need-food conception. This sets up the NT's paradox: in the Eucharist, the relationship is inverted — humans eat and drink (bread and wine as body and blood of Christ, John 6:53-56), not as feeding God but as receiving what God provides. The Lord's Supper is the antithesis of v13: not humans feeding YHWH animal blood, but Christ offering himself as the food and drink that gives eternal life.
14: A theme: "'Bring God a thank-offering as your sacrifice, and keep your vows to the Most High.'" The redirection from animal sacrifice to todah (thank-offering, praise) is the psalm's key constructive move: the sacrifice God wants is gratitude and covenant faithfulness, not animal meat. Hebrews 13:15-16 applies this to the post-Christ community: "through Jesus, therefore, let us continually offer to God a sacrifice of praise — the fruit of lips that openly profess his name. And do not forget to do good and to share with others, for with such sacrifices God is pleased." The thank-offering of Psalms 50:14 is the NT's sacrificial practice — offered "through Jesus."
15: A revelation of God: "'Call on me when trouble comes; I will rescue you, and you will honor me.'" The divine invitation — call, receive rescue, honor — is the covenantal pattern of prayer. Romans 10:12-13 applies this pattern to Christ: "the same Lord is Lord of all and richly blesses all who call on him, for, 'Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved.'" (citing Joel 2:32). The "name of the LORD" on whom all may call in v15 is the name that is "above every name" given to Christ (Philippians 2:9). The rescue promised in Psalms 50:15 is the salvation offered in the name of Jesus Christ.
16: A theme: "But to the wicked, God says: 'What right do you have to quote my laws, or to take my covenant on your lips?'" The critique of selective covenant-membership — claiming the covenant verbally while violating it practically — is what Jesus addresses throughout the Sermon on the Mount and in his critique of the Pharisees (Matthew 23:3: "do as they say, but not as they do"). Romans 2:17-24 makes the same argument that Psalms 50:16-21 makes: "you rely on the law and boast in God... you who preach against stealing, do you steal?" The lip-covenant that the heart denies is the religious failure that Christ's teaching consistently exposes.
17: A theme: "'You despise correction and fling my words aside.'" The contempt for divine discipline (mûsār) — the educational instruction that YHWH uses to shape covenant character — is what Hebrews 12:5-6 identifies as a spiritual failure: "do not make light of the Lord's discipline... because the Lord disciplines the one he loves." Christ is both the source of this discipline (Hebrews 12:7-11) and its model: "although he was a son, he learned obedience from what he suffered" (Hebrews 5:8). The word-flinging of Psalms 50:17 is the opposite of the obedience that Christ embodied.
18: A theme: "'When you see a thief you go along with him, and you make yourself at home with adulterers.'" Social complicity in sin — the tacit endorsement of others' violations — is what Romans 1:32 identifies as the depth of moral corruption: "although they know God's righteous decree that those who do such things deserve death, they not only continue to do these very things but also approve of those who practice them." Christ's holiness is the antithesis: he ate with sinners (which the Pharisees attacked) but not to endorse their sin — rather to call them to repentance (Luke 5:31-32). Associating with sinners without approving their sin is the gospel pattern Christ modeled.
19: A theme: "'You let your mouth run loose into evil, and your tongue is busy weaving lies.'" The unbridled tongue — speech as the avenue for evil rather than blessing — is the failure that James 3:1-12 addresses at length: "the tongue is a fire, a world of evil among the parts of the body." Christ is the Word who embodies perfect speech: "no deceit was found in his mouth" (1 Peter 2:22, citing Isaiah 53:9); "gracious words came from his lips" (Luke 4:22). The contrast between the lying tongue of Psalms 50:19 and the truthful Word made flesh is total.
20: A theme: "'You sit and talk against your own brother; you slander your mother's son.'" Covenantal betrayal within the family — the slander of one's kin — is the social violation that Matthew 18:15-17 addresses with the community-discipline procedure. But the most extreme instance of brother-against-brother in the biblical narrative is the betrayal of Jesus by Judas (John 13:18, citing Psalms 41:9: "even my close friend, whom I trusted... has lifted up his heel against me"). The slander and betrayal of v20 find their darkest expression in the passion narrative.
21: A revelation of God: "'You did all these things, and I said nothing. You concluded I must be just like you. But now I rebuke you and lay everything out before you.'" The dangerous misreading of divine patience as divine approval — "God must be like me" — is the theological error that the cross exposes definitively. Romans 2:4-5: "do you presume on the riches of his kindness, forbearance and patience, not knowing that God's kindness is intended to lead you to repentance? But because of your stubbornness... you are storing up wrath." John 14:9 inverts the error: "whoever has seen me has seen the Father." The cross is the definitive rebuttal of "I thought God was like me" — he is holy, and the cost of holiness meeting sin was borne by his Son.
22: A theme: "'Take this seriously, you who have forgotten God, or I will tear you apart, and no one will rescue you.'" The warning of unrescuable divine judgment against those who forget God is the Old Covenant's starkest ultimatum. Luke 13:3, 5 (Jesus: "unless you repent, you too will all perish") carries the same urgency. Hebrews 2:3 makes the logic of urgency explicit in Christ: "how shall we escape if we ignore so great a salvation?" The one who "will tear apart" the unrepentant in Psalms 50:22 is the same one who says in Matthew 10:28: "be afraid of the One who can destroy both soul and body in hell."
23: A revelation of God: "'Whoever brings thanksgiving as their sacrifice glorifies me; to the one who walks a right path I will show the salvation of God.'" The psalm closes with its constructive vision: praise-as-sacrifice and covenant-walking as the path to divine salvation (yēša'). The Hebrew yēša' (salvation) is the root from which the name Yēšûaʿ (Jesus) derives. "I will show them the salvation of God" — in Christ, God has not merely shown salvation but embodied it. The todah sacrifice that glorifies God in v23 is what the NT community offers "through Jesus" (Hebrews 13:15), the one whose name means "YHWH saves."
